title: Virginia Lee: notes on a career
text:
Section Title: Virginia Lee: notes on a career
Content:
This attitude of getting things done without following preset directions could be credited for Lee’s scientific success. Over the course of three decades, Lee and her husband, [John Trojanowski](http://www.med.upenn.edu/apps/faculty/index.php/c504/p17800 "Faculty profile Web page for John Trojanowski in the Perleman School of Medicine at the University of Pennsylvania") , co-director of the center, have spearheaded efforts to identify some of the proteins involved in Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, frontotemporal dementia and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, known as ALS or Lou Gehrig’s disease. “Science is so exciting now. I would have never imagined, as a high-school girl or even as a postdoc, where I am right now,” says Lee. “It’s a very interesting journey for me.”

Section Title: Virginia Lee: notes on a career > “Couldn’t wait to get out” > Philadelphia
Content:
The M.B.A. training comes in handy in running a research team of up to 50 people. Lee and Trojanowski work with clinicians and help them store their data and bank samples, run a drug-discovery program, and pursue basic science research. Organizing and managing all that requires implementing an infrastructure, which Lee says her business education taught her to do. But more importantly, Lee says, the M.B.A. has helped her think like a small-business owner. “Running your own lab is like running a mom-and-pop shop,” she says. “You get money. You have to pay bills. You have to order supplies and so on. On the small scale, it’s not as important, but when it’s on the large scale, any kind of business training and organization skills, as well as knowing about finances, is important to running a big lab.”
Virginia Lee being interviewed by WTXF-TV Fox 29 News in Philadelphia

Section Title: Virginia Lee: notes on a career
Content:
How the neurobiologist charted a path from piano bench to lab bench
By Rajendrani Mukhopadhyay
June 28, 2013
“I never really had a role model,” says [Virginia M-Y Lee](http://www.med.upenn.edu/apps/faculty/index.php/g275/p12536 "Faculty profile Web page for Virginia M-Y Lee in the Perleman School of Medicine of the University of Pennsylvania") , director at the [Center for Neurodegenerative Disease Research](http://www.med.upenn.edu/cndr/ "Home page for the Center for Neurodegenerative Disease Research at the University of Pennsylvania") at the University of Pennsylvania, pausing to reflect on her unusual career path. “I made decisions based on a number of factors — of where I wanted to go and live, what I wanted to see and what I wanted to learn. Then I just went and did it.”
